title: The First Robot Movie: Watch a Newly Discovered Georges Mûˋliû´s Film from 1897
description:
in Film, History |   	March 4th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

MetropôÙoôÙlis,ô ForôÙbidôÙden PlanôÙet, 2001: A Space Odyssey, Star Wars, Blade RunôÙner,
body:
in Film, History |   	March 4th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

MetropôÙoôÙlis,ô ForôÙbidôÙden PlanôÙet, 2001: A Space Odyssey, Star Wars, Blade RunôÙner,ô The TerôÙmiôÙnaôÙtor,ô Short CirôÙcuit, RoboôÙCop, Ghost in the Shell,ô The Iron Giant, WALLãE, Ex MachiôÙna: there is a parôÙalôÙlel hisôÙtoôÙry of cinôÙeôÙma to be told entireôÙly through its robots.ô That such a hisôÙtoôÙry must begin with the work of Georges Mûˋliû´s may not come as a surôÙprise, givôÙen that he inventôÙed so many of the techôÙniques of sciôÙence-ficôÙtion filmôÙmakôÙing. But until recentôÙly, we didôÙnãt actuôÙalôÙly know that the cinôÙeôÙma pioôÙneer who ãinventôÙed everyôÙthingã ever put a robot onscreen. The eviôÙdence turned up among a colôÙlecôÙtion of ãold and batôÙteredã reels of film that were ãfrom before World War I and had been shutôÙtled around from baseôÙments to barns to garages and had just been dropped off at the Library.ã
So writes the Library of ConôÙgressã Neely TuckôÙer, who goes on to describe the action of one of the films involvôÙing ãa magiôÙcian and a robot batôÙtling it out in slapôÙstick fashôÙion. It took a bit, but then the gasp of realôÙizaôÙtion: They were lookôÙing at ãGugusse and the AutomaôÙton,ã a long-lost film by the iconôÙic French filmôÙmakôÙer Georges Mûˋliû´s at his Star Film comôÙpaôÙny.ã
Mûˋliû´s himôÙself plays the magiôÙcian, who ãwinds up an automaôÙton dressed like the famous clown PierôÙrot, which is standôÙing on a pedestal. Once wound up, the clown begins to beat the magiôÙcian with his walkôÙing stick. The magiôÙcian retalôÙiôÙates by getôÙting a huge sledgeôÙhamôÙmer and bashôÙing the automaôÙton over the head, with each blow seemôÙing to shrink it in half, until it is just a small doll.ã
In just 45 secôÙonds, this simôÙple film would have astonôÙished audiôÙences back in 1897 ã and indeed retains the powôÙer to impress, proôÙvidôÙed you conôÙsidôÙer that none of the techôÙniques to realôÙize its effects were wideôÙly known before Mûˋliû´s attemptôÙed them. He did so five years before ãA Trip to the Moon,ã a hugeôÙly ambiôÙtious cinôÙeôÙmatôÙic endeavôÙor by comôÙparôÙiôÙson, and by far the sinôÙgle film that best repôÙreôÙsents his legaôÙcy.ã Yet it and Gugusse and the AutomaôÙton are clearôÙly the work of the same artist-invenôÙtor, one who posôÙsessed that rare comôÙbiôÙnaôÙtion of techôÙniôÙcal know-how and artisôÙtic darôÙing, and who underôÙstood the need for an organôÙic relaôÙtionôÙship between specôÙtaôÙcle and narôÙraôÙtive. Not that either the specôÙtaôÙcle or the narôÙraôÙtive are highôÙly evolved at this stage, but, as Mûˋliû´s may have susôÙpectôÙed, the cinôÙeôÙma of robots has as long an evoôÙluôÙtion ahead of it as automaôÙta themôÙselves.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
Watch 194 Films by Georges Mûˋliû´s, the FilmôÙmakôÙer Who ãInventôÙed EveryôÙthingã (All in ChronoôÙlogôÙiôÙcal Order)
How Georges Mûˋliû´s A Trip to the Moon Became the First Sci-Fi Film & Changed CinôÙeôÙma ForôÙevôÙer (1902)
The Word ãRobotã OrigôÙiôÙnatôÙed in a Czech Play in 1921: DisôÙcovôÙer Karel áapekãs Sci-Fi Play R.U.R. (a.k.a. Rossumãs UniôÙverôÙsal Robots)
Fritz Lang First DepictôÙed ArtiôÙfiôÙcial IntelôÙliôÙgence on Film in MetropôÙoôÙlis (1927), and It FrightôÙened PeoôÙple Even Then
Watch ãThe Birth of the Robot,ã Len Lyeãs SurôÙreôÙal 1935 Stop-Motion AniôÙmaôÙtion
Watch the Sci-Fi Short Film ãIãm Not a Robotã: WinôÙner of a 2025 AcadôÙeôÙmy Award
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Download 60,000 Works of Art from the National Gallery, Including Masterpieces by Van Gogh, Gauguin, Rembrandt & More
description: As a young amaôÙteur painter and future art school dropout, I freôÙquentôÙly found myself hauntôÙed by the faces of two artists, that famousôÙly odd c
body:
As a young amaôÙteur painter and future art school dropout, I freôÙquentôÙly found myself hauntôÙed by the faces of two artists, that famousôÙly odd couôÙple from my favorite art hisôÙtoôÙry novelizationãand Kirk DouôÙglas role and Iggy Pop songãLust for Life. VinôÙcent van Gogh and Paul GauôÙguin, above and below respecôÙtiveôÙly, the torôÙmentôÙed Dutch fanatôÙic and burly French bullyãhow, I still wonôÙder, could such a pair have ever co-existôÙed, howôÙevôÙer briefly? How could such beauôÙtiôÙfulôÙly skewed visions of life have existôÙed at all?
Van Gogh and Gauguinãs sevôÙerôÙal self-porôÙtraits still inspire wonôÙder. My younger self had the luxôÙuôÙry of seeôÙing these parôÙticôÙuôÙlar two up close and in perôÙson at the NationôÙal Gallery of Art in WashôÙingôÙton, DC: Van Goghãs gaunt and piercôÙing visôÙage, Gauguinãs sneerôÙing self-parôÙoôÙdy.
Now, thanks to the wonôÙders of digôÙiôÙtal techôÙnolôÙoôÙgy, my oldôÙer self, and yours, can view and downôÙload high-resôÙoôÙluôÙtion phoôÙtos of both paintôÙings, and over 60,000 more from the museumãs vast holdôÙings, through NGA Images, ãa reposôÙiôÙtoôÙry of digôÙiôÙtal images of the colôÙlecôÙtions of the NationôÙal Gallery of Art.ã

There youãll find works by anothôÙer obsesôÙsive Dutch self-porôÙtraitist, RemôÙbrandt van Rijn, such as the lush 1659 paintôÙing below. Youãll find paintôÙings from the heroes of the varôÙiôÙous RenaisôÙsances and French ImpresôÙsionôÙism, from moveôÙments modôÙern and coloôÙnial, pasôÙtoral and urban. The colôÙlecôÙtion is dizzyôÙing, and a lover of art could easôÙiôÙly lose hours sortôÙing through it, savôÙing ãopen access digôÙiôÙtal images up to 3000 pixôÙels each [ãÎ] availôÙable free of charge for downôÙload and use.ã The purôÙpose of NGA Images is ãto facilôÙiôÙtate learnôÙing, enrichôÙment, enjoyôÙment, and exploôÙration,ã and thereãs no doubt that it satôÙisôÙfies all of those goals and then some.

Browse the varôÙiôÙous colôÙlecôÙtions, includôÙing one devotôÙed to self-porôÙtraits. ConôÙduct advanced searchôÙes, if you have more knowlôÙedge of the Galleryãs many treaôÙsures. You are the curaôÙtor! And the lucky benôÙeôÙfiôÙciaôÙry of the NationôÙal Galleryãs benefôÙiôÙcence. While I can tell you from expeôÙriôÙence that itãs nothôÙing like standôÙing face to face with these paintôÙings in their in-real-life dimenôÙsions, texôÙtures, lines, and colorsãdespite the throngs of disôÙinôÙterôÙestôÙed touristsãitãs at least a close secôÙond. And for stuôÙdents and eduôÙcaôÙtors of the visuôÙal arts, NGA Images offers an opporôÙtuôÙniôÙty like no othôÙer to view and share great works of art often hidôÙden away from even the museumãs visôÙiôÙtors. Enter the colôÙlecôÙtion here.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2014.
If you would like to sign up for Open Cultureãs free email newsletôÙter,ô please find it here. Itãs a great way to see our new posts, all bunôÙdled in one email, each day.
If you would like to supôÙport the misôÙsion of Open CulôÙture, conôÙsidôÙer makôÙingô a donaôÙtion to our site. Itãs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions will help us conôÙtinôÙue proôÙvidôÙing the best free culôÙturôÙal and eduôÙcaôÙtionôÙal mateôÙriôÙals to learnôÙers everyôÙwhere. You can conôÙtribute through PayôÙPal, PatreôÙon, and VenôÙmo (@openculture). Thanks!
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:

Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.ô

title: Rome in 1890 Captured in Color Photographs: The Colosseum, Forum, Trevi Fountain & More
description: For almost two hunôÙdred years, EngôÙlish genôÙtleôÙmen could not conôÙsidôÙer their eduôÙcaôÙtion comôÙplete until they had takôÙen the ãGrand Tourã of Eu
body:
For almost two hunôÙdred years, EngôÙlish genôÙtleôÙmen could not conôÙsidôÙer their eduôÙcaôÙtion comôÙplete until they had takôÙen the ãGrand Tourã of Europe, usuôÙalôÙly culôÙmiôÙnatôÙing in Naples, ãragaôÙmufôÙfin capôÙiôÙtal of the ItalôÙian south,ã writes Ian ThomôÙson atô The SpecôÙtaôÙtor. Italy was usuôÙalôÙlyô the priôÙmaôÙry focus, such that Samuel JohnôÙson remarked in 1776, perôÙhaps with some irony, ãa man who has not been to Italy is always conôÙscious of an infeôÙriôÙorôÙiôÙty.ã The RomanôÙtic poets famousôÙly wrote of their EuroôÙpean sojourns: ShelôÙley, Byron, WordsworthãÎ each has his own ãGrand Tourã stoôÙry.

ShelôÙley, who travôÙeled with his wife Mary GodôÙwin and her stepôÙsisôÙter Claire ClairôÙmont, did not go to Italy, howôÙevôÙer. And Byron sailed the MediterôÙranean on his Grand Tour, forced away from most of Europe by the NapoleonôÙic wars. But in 1817, he jourôÙneyed to Rome, where he wrote the Fourth CanôÙto of Childe Haroldãs PilôÙgrimôÙage:
Oh Rome! my counôÙtry! city of the soul!
The orphans of the heart must turn to thee,
Lone mothôÙer of dead empires! And conôÙtrol
In their shut breasts their petôÙty misôÙery. 
For the travôÙelôÙing artist and philosoôÙpher, ãItaly,ã ThomôÙson writes, ãpreôÙsentôÙed a civôÙiôÙlizaôÙtion in ruins,ã and we can see in all RomanôÙtic writôÙing the tremenôÙdous influôÙence visions of Rome and PomôÙpeii had on genôÙtleôÙmen poets like Byron. The Grand Tour, and jourôÙneys like it, perôÙsistôÙed until the 1840s, when railôÙroads ãspelled the end of soliôÙtary arisôÙtoôÙcratôÙic travôÙel.ã
But even decades afterôÙward, we can see Rome (and Venice) the way Byron mightô have seen itãand almost, even, in full colôÙor. As we stepô into the visôÙtas of these postôÙcards from 1890, we are far closôÙer to Byron than we are to the Rome of our day, before Mussoliniãs monôÙuôÙments, notoôÙriôÙous snarls of Roman trafôÙfic, and throngs of tourists.

ãThese postôÙcards of the ancient landôÙmarks of Rome,ã writes MashôÙable, ãwere proôÙducedãÎ using the PhoôÙtochrom process, which adds preôÙcise graôÙdaôÙtions of artiôÙfiôÙcial colôÙor to black and white phoôÙtos.ã InventôÙed by Swiss printôÙer Orell GessôÙner FusôÙsli, the process involved creôÙatôÙing lithôÙoôÙgraphôÙic stone from the negativesããUp to 15 difôÙferôÙent tintôÙed stones could be involved in the proôÙducôÙtion of a sinôÙgle picôÙture, but the result was remarkôÙably lifeôÙlike colôÙor at a time when true colôÙor phoôÙtogôÙraôÙphy was still in its infanôÙcy.ã

The Library of ConôÙgress hosts forty eight of these images in their online catôÙaôÙlog, all downôÙloadôÙable as high qualôÙiôÙty jpegs or tiffs, and many, like the stunôÙning image of the ColosôÙseôÙum at the topô (see the inteôÙriôÙor here), feaôÙturôÙing a pre-PhoôÙtocrom black and white print as well.

Aside from a rare street scene, with an urban milieu lookôÙing very much from the 1890s, the phoôÙtographsô are void of crowds. In the foreôÙground of the TriôÙumphal Arch furôÙther upô we see a soliôÙtary woman with a basôÙket of proôÙduce on her head. In the image of San LorenôÙzo, above, a tiny figôÙure walks away from the camôÙera.

In most of these imagesãwith their dreamôÙlike colorationãwe can imagôÙine Rome the way it looked not only in 1890, but also how it might have looked to bored arisôÙtoôÙcrats in the 17thô and 18thô centuriesãand to pasôÙsionôÙate RomanôÙtic poets in the earôÙly 19th,ô a place of raw natôÙurôÙal grandeur and subôÙlime man-made decay. See the Library of ConôÙgress online catôÙaôÙlog to view and downôÙload all forty-eight of these postôÙcards.

Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2016.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:

BeauôÙtiôÙful, ColôÙor PhoôÙtographs of Paris TakôÙen a CenôÙtuôÙry Agoãat the BeginôÙning of World War I & the End ofô La Belle ûpoque

Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.ô

title: The Adventures of Prince Achmed, the Oldest Surviving Animated Feature Film, Is Now in the Public Domain (1926)
description:
in Animation, History |   	March 3rd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	


Die AbenôÙteuer des Prinzen Achmed, orô The AdvenôÙtures of Prince Achmed, lays
body:
in Animation, History |   	March 3rd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	


Die AbenôÙteuer des Prinzen Achmed, orô The AdvenôÙtures of Prince Achmed, lays fair claim to being the earôÙliôÙest aniôÙmatôÙed feaôÙture film in exisôÙtence.ô If we do grant it that title, it beats the next conôÙtender by more than a decade. Whileô Princeô Achmedô came out a cenôÙtuôÙry ago, in 1926, Snow White and the SevôÙen Dwarfs, whose proôÙducôÙtion was presided over by a cerôÙtain Walt DisôÙney, didôÙnãt reach theôÙaters until 1937. The latôÙter picôÙture holds great disôÙtincôÙtion in the hisôÙtoôÙry of cinôÙeôÙma, of course, not least that of being the first feaôÙture made with cel aniôÙmaôÙtion: the domôÙiôÙnant techôÙnique throughôÙout most of the twenôÙtiôÙeth cenôÙtuôÙry, and one whose digôÙiôÙtal replaceôÙment has been lamentôÙed by clasôÙsic aniôÙmaôÙtion enthuôÙsiôÙasts. But the quivôÙerôÙing silôÙhouôÙettes of Prince Achmedô show an alterôÙnaôÙtive.
The makôÙing ofô Snow White was, by the stanôÙdards of the day, a vast underôÙtakôÙing, requirôÙing DisôÙney to marôÙshal artisôÙtic and indusôÙtriôÙal resources at a scale then unknown in aniôÙmaôÙtion. Prince Achmed, by conôÙtrast, owes its exisôÙtence mostôÙly to the work of one woman: Lotte Reiniger, who first learned the craft ofô scherenôÙschnitte silôÙhouôÙette-makôÙing as a litôÙtle girl in Berlin.
ScherenôÙschnitte was inspired by what was thought to be ancient ChiôÙnese arts of paper-cutôÙting and pupôÙpetry, but when watched today,ô Prince Achmedô or the othôÙer aniôÙmaôÙtions Reiniger creôÙatôÙed bring more readôÙiôÙly to mind traôÙdiôÙtionôÙal Javanese wayangô kulit shadôÙow pupôÙpet theôÙater: an aesôÙthetôÙic that,ô in a sense, suits the source mateôÙrôÙiôÙal ideôÙalôÙly.
The episodes that conôÙstiôÙtuteô Prince Achmedãs narôÙraôÙtive are drawn in large part from One ThouôÙsand and One Nights, a text whose cenôÙturies-long evoôÙluôÙtion bears the marks of not just many disôÙtinct culôÙtures across Asia and the MidôÙdle East,ô but also those of more draôÙmatôÙic transôÙforôÙmaôÙtion through its folkôÙtalesã culôÙturôÙal transôÙpoôÙsiôÙtion into French, then othôÙer EuroôÙpean lanôÙguages.ô Whatô Reiniger brings to enchantôÙiôÙng handôÙmade lifeô isnãt any parôÙticôÙuôÙlar place at any parôÙticôÙuôÙlar time, but rather an eleôÙgant, mysôÙteôÙriôÙous, quite litôÙerôÙalôÙly arabesque realm that nevôÙer realôÙly existôÙed. In othôÙer words, Prince Achmedô takes place in what can only be called the OriôÙent ã which, now that the film has fallôÙen into the pubôÙlic domain, we can all visôÙit whenôÙevôÙer we like. And if such visôÙits hapôÙpen to inspire a new genôÙerôÙaôÙtion of Lotte Reinigers in this world of marôÙket-researched mega-budôÙget aniôÙmaôÙtion, so much the betôÙter.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
The First AniôÙmatôÙed FeaôÙture Film: The AdvenôÙtures of Prince Achmed by Lotte Reiniger (1926)
The GroundôÙbreakôÙing SilôÙhouôÙette AniôÙmaôÙtions of Lotte Reiniger: CinôÙderelôÙla, Hansel and GreôÙtel, and More
AniôÙmaôÙtion PioôÙneer Lotte Reiniger Adapts Mozartãs The MagôÙic Flute into an All-SilôÙhouôÙette Short Film (1935)
The AniôÙmaôÙtions That Changed CinôÙeôÙma: The GroundôÙbreakôÙing LegaôÙcies of Prince Achmed, AkiôÙra, The Iron Giant & More
Watch the OldôÙest JapanôÙese AniôÙme Film, Junãichi Kéuchiãs The Dull Sword (1917)
The BeauôÙtiôÙful AnarôÙchy of the EarôÙliôÙest AniôÙmatôÙed CarôÙtoons: Explore an Archive with 200+ EarôÙly AniôÙmaôÙtions
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Tshepiso Moropa Splices Setswana Folklore and Dreams into Archival Collages
description: Using personal and archival images, Tshepiso Moropa cuts and splices delicate collages that consider the ever-evolving nature of the stories we te
body:
Using personal and archival images, Tshepiso Moropa cuts and splices delicate collages that consider the ever-evolving nature of the stories we tell. The self-taught artist draws on her background in psychology and linguistics as she plumbs African archives and oral histories, reinterpreting her findings through minimal, yet weighty compositions.
Moropa often grounds her works within dinaane and ditoro, Setswana lore and dreams, respectively. ãEach folktale carries a unique blend of history, cultural values, and human experience, serving as a wellspring of inspiration,ã she says in a statement. ãMy work is informed by the timeless wisdom, moral lessons, and magical elements found within Sestwana stories.ã
ãThe Joyã (2023), digital collage on archival matte, 42 x 29.7 centimeters
Fusing digital and analog techniques, the artist assembles surreal scenes with precise lines and ample negative space. Birds figure prominently and serve as spiritual guides as in pieces like ãThe Joy,ã which the artist describes as referencing the following story: ãWhen the girl drifted into the darkness, the ancestors came to her disguised as birds, their wings turning into gentle threads that lifted her away from harm.ã
Other works are more personal, including ãHiding in Plain Sight.ã Depicting an older woman floating above a wooden home, the piece alludes to one of Moropaãs dreams in which her grandmother appeared in the sky, hovering above the artist and her twin sister.
Moropa is based in Johannesburg and represented by THK Gallery in Cape Town. This April, her work will go on view in a group exhibition at Museum Rietberg in Zurich, followed by a presentation at the Biel/Bienne Festival of Photography in Switzerland. Keep up with her practice on Instagram.
ãHiding in Plain Sightã (2025, collage matte paper, 40 x 40 centimeters
ãRiding Shotgunã (2025), collage, 40 x 40 centimeters
ãThe Flying Girlã (2023), collage, 40 x 40 centimeters
ãThe Ride Homeã (2024), Digital print on Hanhemû¥hle Photo Rag, 40 x 40 centimeters

title: Masayoshi Matsumoto Meticulously Transforms Balloons into Expressive Critters
description:
All images courtesy of the artist, shared with permission
March 3, 2026


For Masayoshi Matsumoto, also known as Isopresso Balloon, a simple dog-shap
body:
All images courtesy of the artist, shared with permission
March 3, 2026


For Masayoshi Matsumoto, also known as Isopresso Balloon, a simple dog-shaped balloon animal is utter childãs play. His elaborate constructions combine a range of colors and can take on virtually any shape. From meticulously textured squids to demure gophers to stout tropical birds, the artist conjures playful and expressive animals from stretchy rubber and air. Lately, heãs been particularly interested in birds, expertly twisting beaks and tail feathers into recognizable species like mallards, swans, and a bright kingfisher.
See more on Instagram, and try your hand at balloon art of your own with a range of tutorials on YouTube. Heãs currently working on a few new videos geared specifically toward beginner balloon sculptors.

title: Rediscover a Rembrandt After More than Six Decades in Hiding
description: In 1898, Amsterdamãs Stedelijk Museum staged an exhibition of paintings by renowned Dutch Golden Age artist Rembrandt (1606-1669). Included in thi
body:
In 1898, Amsterdamãs Stedelijk Museum staged an exhibition of paintings by renowned Dutch Golden Age artist Rembrandt (1606-1669). Included in this show was a 23-by-19-inch oil painting titled ãVision of Zacharias in the Temple,ã which was completed in 1633, relatively early in the artistãs career. Fast-forward to 1960, and the work was deemed to have not actually been made by Rembrandt. Despite that in the past it had been catalogued as part of his oeuvre, that was no longer the case. So, a private collector purchased it in 1961, from which point on, it remained out of sightãuntil now.
Experts and conservators at the Rijksmuseum, which was recently granted the opportunity to reassess the painting by its owner, spent two years investigating the pigments, structural materials, Rembrandtãs signature and date, the overall style, and the choice of subject matter. Techniques included X-raying the paint and conducting dendrochronology tests on the wooden panels to get a sense of age.

Macro scans also revealed insights into the artistãs creative and technical choices as he worked. ãAn important piece of evidence that the painting is by Rembrandt lies in the changes he made during the painting process,ã the museum says. ãFor example, the censer [an incense burner] on the altar was originally larger. Such adjustments are characteristic of an artist who is experimenting and refining his work. A copyist, by contrast, would have reproduced the model exactly, without making alterations.ã
The scene depicts the high priest Zacharias at the moment just before the archangel Gabriel arrives to tell him that he and his wife will have a son, John the Baptist. Though dim in photographs, a light in the upper right corner of the canvas indicates Gabrielãs imminent arrival. In this charged moment, Rembrandt uses a narrative device known in Dutch as ãstaetveranderinge,ã which translates to ãchanges of state.ã Itãs an element Rembrandt often incorporated in his paintings around this time.
The Rijksmuseum also recently spent five years thoroughly researching and restoring ãThe Night Watch.ã If youãre in Amsterdam, ãVision of Zacharias in the Templeã goes on view today at the Rijksmuseum, where it will remain on long-term loan. Find more on the museumãs website.

schema.headline: Rediscover a Rembrandt After More than Six Decades in Hiding
